questions.q: What does IEEE 802.15.4 primarily focus on?
questions.exp: IEEE 802.15.4 defines the PHY and MAC layers for low-rate wireless personal area networks (LR-WPANs) — the foundation for ZigBee, Thread, and 6LoWPAN.

questions.q: IEEE 802.15.4 defines the operation of which network layers?
questions.exp: IEEE 802.15.4 defines the Physical (PHY) layer (modulation, radio transceivers) and the Data Link layer (specifically the MAC sublayer for channel access).

questions.q: IEEE 802.15.4 is a basis for which popular IoT communication protocol?
questions.exp: Both ZigBee and Thread use IEEE 802.15.4 as their underlying PHY and MAC layer standard. WiFi uses 802.11, LTE is a cellular standard, and Bluetooth is unrelated.

questions.q: Which of the following are used by IEEE 802.15.4?
questions.exp: IEEE 802.15.4 uses Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) for the physical layer and CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance) for channel access. FHSS and CSMA/CD are not used.

questions.q: Which type of modulation is used in IEEE 802.15.4?
questions.exp: IEEE 802.15.4 uses DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum) modulation, which spreads the signal across a wider bandwidth for robustness against interference.

questions.q: What kind of network topology is supported by IEEE 802.15.4?
questions.exp: IEEE 802.15.4 supports Star, Point-to-Point, and Mesh topologies. Bus topology is not supported.

questions.q: Which of the following frames are required to be transmitted using CSMA/CA?
questions.exp: In IEEE 802.15.4, only Data Frames use CSMA/CA for channel access. Acknowledgements and Beacons are sent immediately without contention.

questions.q: The full form of 6LoWPAN is ...
opts: 6 Low Power Wide Personal Area Network
IPv6 over Low-Power Wide Area Networks
6 Low Power Wireless Personal Area Network
IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks
questions.exp: 6LoWPAN stands for IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks — it adapts IPv6 packets to run over IEEE 802.15.4 radio links.

questions.q: 6LowPAN technology sits on top of ...
questions.exp: 6LoWPAN sits on top of IEEE 802.15.4, adapting IPv6 to work within the constraints of low-power 802.15.4 radio links (small frame size, limited bandwidth).

questions.q: 6LowPAN uses traditional IPv6 as its networking layer.
questions.exp: False — 6LoWPAN does NOT use traditional IPv6 directly. It compresses IPv6 headers and adapts packets to fit within the small frame size of IEEE 802.15.4.

questions.q: 6LowPAN is the same as IEEE802.15.4
questions.exp: False — IEEE 802.15.4 defines the PHY and MAC layers. 6LoWPAN is an adaptation layer above 802.15.4 that enables IPv6 communication over it.

questions.q: Which of the following are not true about 6LowPAN?
opts: 6LowPAN is based on IPv4 and IPv6 protocols for transmitting packets
6LowPAN has the capability of optional mesh routing in the data link layer
6LowPAN is a networking layer protocol for Internet of Things
None of the statements are false.
questions.exp: 6LoWPAN is based on IPv6 only — not IPv4. It is an IPv6 adaptation layer specifically designed for low-power IEEE 802.15.4 networks.

questions.q: If the 16-bit address assigned to a device in a 6LowPAN network is 4EA1, what is the IPv6 link local address?
questions.exp: Without a PAN ID, the 6LoWPAN link-local address is formed as FE80::/64 with the 16-bit short address: FE80::4EA1.

questions.q: If the 16-bit address assigned to a device in a 6LowPAN network is 4EA1, what is the IPv6 link local address? Assume the PAN ID is 5678.
questions.exp: In 6LoWPAN with a PAN ID, the link-local address embeds the PAN ID and short address: FE80::5678:4EA1.

questions.q: Assume that a user wants to transmit bit 0 using Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum. The user using "XOR" to generate the same. What is the transmitted signal (in binary) if the chipping sequence is 011011000101?
questions.exp: Bit 0 XOR each chip = 0 XOR chip = chip unchanged. So transmitted signal = chipping sequence = 011011000101.

questions.q: Assume that a user wants to transmit bit 1 using Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum. The user using "XOR" to generate the same. What is the transmitted signal (in binary) if the chipping sequence is 101011010011?
questions.exp: Bit 1 XOR each chip = 1 XOR chip = complement. 101011010011 → complement = 010100101100.

questions.q: Assume Bob is using DSSS to transmit his data to Alice. Bob decided to use the chipping sequence 10110100110 to transmit his bits. Bob transmitted 330 bits to Alice. Using this information, calculate how many actual bits represent Bob's data?
questions.exp: Chipping sequence length = 11 bits. Actual data bits = Total transmitted ÷ Chips per bit = 330 ÷ 11 = 30 bits.

questions.q: Assume Bob is using DSSS to transmit his data to Alice. Bob decided to use the chipping sequence 101101 to transmit his bits. If the length of the packet is 100 bits, how many bit will Bob actually transmit?
questions.exp: Chipping sequence length = 6. Total transmitted bits = Data bits × Chips per bit = 100 × 6 = 600 bits.

questions.q: Which of the following Full Thread Devices have routing capabilities? (You can select more than one)
questions.exp: Routers, Leaders (a specialised router), and Router Eligible End Devices (REEDs) have routing capabilities. Full End Devices explicitly do not route traffic.

questions.q: Which of the following phases are required for a device to re-attach to a Thread Network? (You can select more than one)
questions.exp: To re-attach to a Thread Network, a device only needs the Attaching phase — it already has its credentials from initial commissioning.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT true about Border Routers in a Thread Network?
opts: Border Router provide services to devices within the Thread Network only.
Border Router can provide connectivity from Thread Network to 802.11 networks.
A REED can be configured as a Border Router in the Thread Network.
There cannot be more than 3 border routers in a Thread Network.
questions.exp: Thread Networks can have multiple Border Routers — there is no limit of 3. Multiple Border Routers improve redundancy and resilience.

questions.q: The size of MTU for an 802.15.4 network is equal to 127 bytes. Of which, 802.15.4 header occupies 25 bytes, security option occupies 21 bytes, IPv6 and UDP compressed headers occupy 10 bytes. Given this situation, choose which of the following application layer payloads (only the size of the payload is shown) can be transmitted over the network. Assume Thread protocol is used for the higher layers.
questions.exp: Available payload = 127 − 25 − 21 − 10 = 71 bytes per frame. Since Thread supports fragmentation and reassembly, payloads larger than 71 bytes (80, 120, 127) can also be transmitted across multiple frames — so all options are valid.

questions.q: Consider an IEEE 802.15.4 network using Beacon-enabled CSMA/CA. Given this, choose which of the following statements are false:
opts: Devices can sleep during the inactive interval of the beacon interval.
Contention Free Period is reserved for real-time services.
Contention Access Period is reserved for real-time services.
Contention Free Perios uses slotted CSMA.
Contention Access Period uses slotted CSMA.
A part of the beacon interval is busy as coordinator requires it to send out MAC command packets.
questions.exp: In a beacon-enabled IEEE 802.15.4 network, the Contention Free Period is reserved for real-time services and uses slotted CSMA/CA. The Contention Access Period is for non-real-time services and uses unslotted CSMA/CA. Devices can sleep during the inactive interval, and the coordinator may use part of the beacon interval to send MAC command packets.

questions.q: If the EUI64 address of a device is 62::1, what would be its link local IPv6 address. Assume 6LowPAN protocol.
questions.exp: In 6LowPAN, the link-local address is formed by embedding the EUI-64 address into the FE80::/64 prefix. The EUI-64 address 62::1 is converted to binary, and the Universal/Local (U/L) bit is set to 1 (indicating a locally administered address). The resulting link-local address is FE80:0:0:0:22:1.

questions.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of Thread?
opts: Devices can easily join the network
There is no single point of failure
All the options are characteristics of Thread
The range of the network is more than enough to cover a home.
questions.exp: While Thread is designed to be easy to join, the process still requires commissioning and authentication steps that may not be considered 'easy' for all users. The other options are true characteristics of Thread: it can support large networks, has no single point of failure due to its mesh architecture, and is designed to cover typical home environments.
